perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless to the Disclosing Party, its affiliates, subsidiaries, successors, assigns, officers, directors, employees, at all times, from and against any and a ll claims, demands, damages, assertions of liability whether civil, criminal, tortuous or of an nature whatsoever, arising out of or pertaining to or resulting from any breach of representations and warranties made by Receiving Party and/or breach o f any provisions of this Agreement, but not limited to any claim from third party pursuant to any act or omission of Receiving Party in the course of discharge of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless to the Disclosing Party, its affiliates, subsidiaries, successors, assigns, officers, directors, employees, from and against any claims, demands, damages, assertions of liability arising out of any breach of representations and warranties made by Receiving Party and/or breach of any provisions of this Agreement, to the extent permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless' to 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless' weakens the obligation. Adding 'to the extent permitted by law' introduces further ambiguity, making the indemnification contingent and less enforceable. The wording could be interpreted to mean that Receiving party will only indemnify within the confines of Indian law, reducing the effectiveness of this clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 124 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 124 defines a 'contract of indemnity' as one party promising to save the other from loss caused to him by the conduct of the promisor himself, or by the conduct of any other person. By using 'endeavor' and adding 'to the extent permitted by law,' the contract creates ambiguity about the scope of the Receiving Party’s promise to indemnify, potentially conflicting with the full protection that a contract of indemnity intends to provide under Section 124.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a clear obligation on the Receiving Party to 'defend, indemnify and hold harmless' the Disclosing Party. The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless' with 'shall endeavor to indemnify and hold harmless' and adding 'to the extent permitted by law'. This introduces ambiguity and makes the indemnification contingent. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this could be interpreted to mean that the Receiving party will only indemnify within the confines of Indian law, reducing the effectiveness of the clause. The Law Explanation says that this change conflicts with the full protection that a contract of indemnity intends to provide under Section 124. Even though Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' message, the Law Explanation still refers to Section 124 which supports the idea of a full indemnification. The changed text, therefore, contradicts the legal intention of a contract of indemnity.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India, and the competent courts in Bangalore shall have jurisdiction in relation thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally guided by the principles of law prevalent in India, and disputes may be submitted to courts in Bangalore.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India' to 'shall be generally guided by the principles of law prevalent in India' significantly weakens the governing law clause. Replacing 'the competent courts in Bangalore shall have jurisdiction' with 'disputes may be submitted to courts in Bangalore' makes the jurisdiction permissive rather than mandatory, creating ambiguity about the enforceability of the agreement under Indian law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act addresses agreements in restraint of legal proceedings. While it permits agreements to refer disputes to arbitration, it generally voids agreements that restrict a party absolutely from enforcing his rights under or in respect of any contract, by the usual legal proceedings in the ordinary tribunals. By using 'generally guided' and weakening the jurisdiction clause, the parties are not bound to the laws of India, creating issues with what laws would govern the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the governing law and jurisdiction clauses, potentially conflicting with the principle that agreements should not unduly restrict legal proceedings. By changing 'shall be governed by' to 'generally guided by,' the contract introduces ambiguity about the applicable law, potentially hindering enforcement. The change from mandatory jurisdiction ('shall have jurisdiction') to permissive ('may be submitted') further weakens the enforceability of the agreement under Indian law. The provided snippets are not relevant.
